metadata.summary: Centralize sentinel errors in apperror, wrap with fmt.Errorf, use errors.Is/As, map errors at transport edge, log once. Enforce with errcheck, errorlint, wrapcheck.

content.title: Go Error Handling Patterns
content.summary: Go error handling convention for services: define sentinel errors in one apperror package; wrap with fmt.Errorf("op: %w", err); check errors with errors.Is/As, never ==; map domain errors to HTTP/gRPC codes only at transport edge; log once at boundary, never log-and-return.

why.p: Bare errors.New("failed") without context makes stack traces useless in production. %v formats without wrapping — errors.Is cannot traverse chain. Logging + returning creates duplicate Sentry events. Centralizing sentinels in apperror makes domain error contract explicit.

list.item: Designing error contracts for new Go services
Refactoring legacy Go code with bare errors
Auditing for double-wrapping or missing errors.Is checks
Mapping domain errors to HTTP status codes
Quick scripts where log.Fatal is acceptable
One-shot migration scripts where error taxonomy is overkill

rule.statement: Wrap every error with operation name using fmt.Errorf("operation_name: %w", err). Use %w not %v.
rule.rationale: %v formats but does not wrap, making errors.Is traversal impossible. %w preserves the chain.

rule.statement: Define sentinel errors in one apperror package per service. Repository wraps low-level errors into domain sentinels.
rule.rationale: Preventing sql.ErrNoRows leak into business logic keeps domain transport-agnostic.

rule.statement: Always use errors.Is to compare sentinels. Never use == to compare errors.
rule.rationale: == fails on wrapped errors. errors.Is traverses the entire chain.

rule.statement: Log errors once at the outermost boundary. Never log-and-return.
rule.rationale: Log-and-return creates 3 Sentry events for one failure through a 3-layer call chain.

template.purpose: Sentinel errors, AppError type, HTTP mapping
Lint config with errcheck, errorlint, wrapcheck

see-also.link: Go blog: Working with Errors in Go 1.13
Cockroach error library with stack traces
